title: Superfund Liability
subtitle: Hazardous Substance Designation
description: The regulatory history of designating PFAS compounds as CERCLA hazardous substances — the legal mechanism that triggers Superfund cleanup authority and makes polluters liable for remediation costs.
currentStatus: Retained. Trump 2 EPA confirmed the PFOA and PFOS CERCLA designations will stand and be defended in court. Enforcement discretion extended to municipalities, water utilities, farmers, and fire departments — liability focused on manufacturers.

timeline.title: Trump 1 EPA opens CERCLA designation rulemaking for PFOA and PFOS
timeline.description: Five days before leaving office, the first Trump administration publishes an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANPRM) seeking public comment on whether to designate PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances. The action begins the formal process — but leaves the actual designation to the incoming administration.

timeline.title: Biden EPA publishes proposed rule to designate PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances
timeline.description: EPA formally proposes designating PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances under Section 102(a) of the law. If finalized, the designation would require facilities to report releases of one pound or more and give EPA authority to compel cleanup and recover costs from responsible parties. The 60-day comment period generates broad industry opposition over liability concerns.

timeline.title: Biden EPA finalizes CERCLA hazardous substance designation for PFOA and PFOS
timeline.description: EPA publishes the final rule designating PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances, effective July 8, 2024. The first synthetic chemicals designated under CERCLA since the law's enactment in 1980. The designation triggers mandatory release reporting at one pound or more and gives EPA authority to pursue cost recovery from polluters at contaminated sites. Industry groups file court challenges immediately.

timeline.title: Industry groups challenge CERCLA designation in D.C. Circuit
timeline.description: The American Chemistry Council, the National Association of Manufacturers, the Appliance Standards Awareness Project, and other industry groups file petitions for review challenging EPA's CERCLA designation of PFOA and PFOS. The challenges argue EPA exceeded its authority and that the designation's broad liability sweep will create perverse incentives, discouraging voluntary cleanup by parties who fear Superfund cost recovery.

timeline.title: Trump 2 EPA announces it will retain PFOA and PFOS CERCLA designation
timeline.description: In a move that surprised industry, EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin announces the Trump administration will keep the CERCLA hazardous substance designations for PFOA and PFOS and defend them in court. EPA simultaneously announces enforcement discretion: the agency will not pursue CERCLA cost recovery against municipalities, water utilities, farmers, landfills, airports, or fire departments — focusing Superfund liability on manufacturers and industrial polluters. The administration frames the decision as protecting communities while holding the chemical industry accountable.

timeline.title: Trump EPA updates PFAS destruction and disposal guidance
timeline.description: EPA releases updated interim guidance on how to safely destroy or dispose of PFAS-containing waste, including aqueous film-forming foam (AFFF). Expands the list of EPA-approved thermal treatment technologies and provides clearer direction on PFAS waste disposal for military installations, airports, and industrial sites — an important complement to the CERCLA designation for cleanup purposes.
